subject: First Year Algebra
instructions: Answer four problems. (If you turn in more, the first four will be graded.) Within reason, you may quote theorems as long as you state them clearly.

problems.text: Give an example of each of the following.
Let \(G\) be a group and let \(g \in G\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define what is meant by the order of \(g\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Let \(g\) have infinite order, and suppose that \(N\) is a finite normal subgroup of \(G\). Prove, from your definition, that the element \(gN \in G/N\) has infinite order.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define what it means for a subset of a group \(G\) to generate \(G\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Let \(G\) be a group, and suppose \(|G| = 2^n\). Prove that there is a subset \(S\) of cardinality \(n\) such that \(S\) generates \(G\).

problems.text: State and prove the Second Isomorphism Theorem for groups.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Let \(S\) be a simple group and suppose that \(S\) acts non-trivially on a finite set \(A\) with \(|A| = n\). Prove that \(|S| \leq n!\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Suppose \(G = A_5\) (the alternating group on five letters) is acting on a set \(A\) in such a way that no element of \(A\) is fixed by every element of \(G\). Suppose that \(|A| = 10\). Prove that \(G\) has at most two orbits in its action on \(A\).

problems.text: State and prove the First Sylow Theorem.
